interactions.summary: Jiang opens from a personal threshold: he is leaving Beijing for Toronto with his sons, has just checked his health, then sees Israel attacking Iran and says the World War III trajectory he predicted appears to be accelerating faster than he imagined.

He gives his biography as an education story: born in China, raised in Toronto, poor family, Yale scholarship, English literature, return to China, and years working across levels of education to promote reform.

He explains that COVID staffing needs pushed him from humanities curriculum director into AP English teaching, where reading great books with Chinese students exposed their lack of historical context.

He turns the course origin into an intellectual program: teaching all of human history made him want a new history that connects the past into story, explains the present, predicts the future, and eventually builds the foundations of psychohistory.

He describes the channel's accidental public turn: lectures uploaded for Chinese students became a rapidly growing global audience, while he remains constrained by family, illness, teaching prep, and limited ability to answer comments.
Jiang says he needs to strengthen philosophy and economics over the summer, especially classical economics, because those are weak points in his current analysis of world events.

He lays out the next curricular sequence: return to Beijing in mid-August, compress the 60-class human-history course into 30 sharper classes, then teach geopolitics by explaining current events and making predictions.

He states his long-term institutional ambition: keep teaching a global audience, develop psychohistory fully, and build a school for liberal arts and humanities that trains future intellectuals, writers, and historians.

He closes by thanking viewers, promising a leaner and cleaner viewing experience, joking about fixing his hair, and committing the summer to deep reflection, learning, and reading before returning in mid-August.

speaker_notes.note: Single-speaker direct-to-camera address; diarized SPEAKER_00 appears to be Jiang throughout.
Single-speaker direct address; no audience questions are spoken in the transcript. References to viewer comments are Jiang summarizing feedback.

claims.claim: Jiang says Israel's attack on Iran confirms the direction he had forecast in previous videos, but the speed of escalation toward World War III is faster and harder than he imagined.

He presents his biography as an education-reform arc: immigrant childhood in Toronto, Yale English literature, return to China, and work across kindergarten, primary, secondary, university, and school-leadership roles.
Chinese students' lack of historical context while reading great books becomes the immediate pedagogical problem that leads Jiang to build a year-long human-history course.
Jiang defines predictive history as true history only if it connects past events into a coherent story, explains the present, and predicts the future.
He says true history would help humanity better organize, better think, and better control its future.
Asimov's Foundation and psychohistory supply Jiang's long-term ambition: to build intellectual foundations for mathematically modeling the past and present in order to predict the future.

The YouTube channel began as a review aid for Chinese students but rapidly became a global audience after growing from roughly 300 subscribers at the beginning of May to about 25,000 by this recording.
Jiang identifies philosophy and classical economics as weak points in his analysis and says he will use the summer to study them more deeply.
claims.temporal_scope: Self-assessment and summer plan stated on 2025-06-13.

claims.claim: When he returns to Beijing in mid-August, Jiang plans to compress the 60-class human-history course into a 30-class version with more material and clearer underlying themes.
claims.temporal_scope: Course plan for mid-August 2025 and the fall term.

claims.claim: Jiang plans a second-semester geopolitics course that analyzes current events, explains why they are happening, and predicts what will happen.
claims.temporal_scope: Planned second semester beginning February after the 2025 fall course.

claims.claim: His long-term ambition is to create a school specializing in liberal arts and humanities that builds the foundations for psychohistory.
Jiang imagines the school as a Plato's Academy or Jedi Temple for training future intellectuals, writers, and historians who can help lead humanity forward.
Viewer comments will shape the next course iteration, which Jiang promises will be leaner, cleaner, and more enjoyable.
claims.temporal_scope: Course-improvement promise for the next school year, stated on 2025-06-13.

signature_moments.moment: The episode begins with blood work, family travel, and the news of Israel attacking Iran collapsing into one historical threshold.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I checked the news and Israel is attacking Iran
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes predictive history personal: the same morning contains mortality, family, and world-war acceleration.

signature_moments.moment: Predictive history is defined as a three-part test: story, explanation, prediction.
signature_moments.source_phrase: connect events of the past into a coherent story... explain the present... predict the future
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the clearest compact definition of the channel's intellectual project in the source.

signature_moments.moment: Asimov's fictional psychohistory becomes Jiang's concrete research ambition.
signature_moments.source_phrase: build intellectual foundations for the possibility of psychohistory
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The source turns the channel from commentary into an announced project of historical modeling.

signature_moments.moment: The channel's audience explosion is described almost as an accident: a student review archive suddenly becomes a global classroom.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explains why this direct address exists and why Jiang has to renegotiate teacher, family, and public intellectual roles.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang publicly marks his own analysis as incomplete because economics and philosophy still need deep repair.
signature_moments.source_phrase: that really right now is the weak point in my analysis
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The admission keeps the predictive-history project from hardening into finished doctrine; it is still a learning program.

signature_moments.moment: The next version of the course is framed as a compression that should make the hidden structure of human history more visible.
signature_moments.source_phrase: see the underlying structures of human history much more clearly
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It states the craft goal behind the revised course: not more facts, but a clearer structural map.

signature_moments.moment: Geopolitics is announced as predictive history applied to live events.
signature_moments.source_phrase: looking at current events trying to analyze why these events are happening and making predictions
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This connects the origin story to the channel's next public mode: real-time explanation and forecast.

signature_moments.moment: The future school is imagined as Plato's Academy fused with a Jedi Temple.
signature_moments.source_phrase: like Plato's Academy... like the Jedi Temple
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The metaphor gives Jiang's institutional ambition its mythic shape: a school for forming future intellectual guardians.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's proposed new history: connect past events into coherent story, explain the present, and predict the future.
An Asimov-inspired ambition to mathematically model the past and present in order to predict the future.
Jiang's name for history that succeeds at story, present explanation, and future prediction.
The planned second-semester course applying Jiang's method to current events, causes, and predictions.
Jiang's analogy for the kind of school he wants to build: an elite intellectual formation site.
Jiang's pop-cultural analogy for training future intellectuals, writers, and historians as guardians of humanity's future.

chronology_notes.note: The Iran/Israel and World War III statement is explicitly dated to 2025-06-13 and references earlier videos without naming them; treat it as a dated update, not a timeless forecast.
Jiang dates the subscriber jump to the past month and says the channel grew from about 300 subscribers at the beginning of May to about 25,000 by 2025-06-13.
The course plan is time-bound: summer 2025 for study and revision, mid-August 2025 for return to Beijing, fall for the 30-class course, and February for the geopolitics course.
Jiang closes by saying he will see viewers in mid-August, reinforcing this as a summer-break planning source rather than a normal lecture.

uncertainty_notes.note: The transcript phrase 'Some of you have suggested anything I've taught' is likely ASR damage; semantic extraction only preserves the clear point that he plans summer reading and reflection.
The transcript phrase 'St. Athos' appears to be ASR damage in the promise of a cleaner viewing experience; semantic extraction does not depend on the phrase.
